channel.title: Sam C Barth - RevOps, AI & CRM Strategy
channel.description: RevOps, AI, and CRM strategy from Sam C Barth.

item.title: SAP's Dremio deal is really a data context play
item.description: SAP buying Dremio is not really a headline about M&A. It is a headline about the part of enterprise AI most teams still skip: the data layer. SAP says the deal is meant to accelerate agentic AI and combine SAP and non-SAP data in real time. That is the right problem. Most AI

item.title: OpenAI's partner network is the real enterprise AI channel
item.description: OpenAI's partner network is the part of this week's news I would watch. It says the company knows enterprise AI is no longer just a model problem. It is a delivery problem. OpenAI says the limiting factor is not model capability anymore. The harder work is finding the right use c

item.title: IBM Bob is the real enterprise AI development story
item.description: IBM Bob is not a coding toy. It is IBM trying to sell enterprise AI around the part of software work that actually costs money: review, validation, modernization, and cleanup. IBM says the bottleneck has moved from writing code to reviewing and validating it. That is the right pr

item.title: ChatGPT Work is the real workflow shift
item.description: OpenAI's July 9 ChatGPT Work announcement is not a chatbot story. It is a workflow story. The company is trying to move the product from answering questions to finishing repeatable work across the apps and files people already use. That is why the pitch matters. OpenAI says ChatG

item.title: GPT-5.6 is a price-per-task story
item.description: GPT-5.6 is not really a hype story. It is a cost story. OpenAI and Microsoft are both pushing the same idea: the win is more useful work for the same spend. OpenAI said GPT-5.6 brings "more intelligence from every token" . That is the right lens. The company also split the family

item.title: IBM and Red Hat are turning open source security into an AI business
item.description: AI makes code faster. That is not the hard part anymore. The hard part is making sure the code can be trusted, patched, and shipped without breaking the system around it. IBM and Red Hat said on July 8 they are commercializing Lightwell, which starts with automated vulnerability

item.title: Microsoft's Frontier Company is the real AI services story
item.description: Microsoft's Frontier Company is not a normal product launch. It is Microsoft admitting that enterprise AI now needs people inside the customer workflow, not just another model to test. In its July 2 announcement , Microsoft said it is making a $2.5 billion investment and embeddin

item.title: OpenAI and Broadcom are pulling AI economics in-house
item.description: OpenAI and Broadcom said on June 24 that they are building Jalapeño, OpenAI's first Intelligence Processor, for LLM inference. That is the part that matters. It is not just a faster chip. It is OpenAI pushing deeper into the infrastructure underneath ChatGPT, Codex, and the API.

item.title: Streaming changed movies, and theaters are rewarding smaller bets
item.description: Streaming changed movies. It did not kill them. It just changed what people will show up for, what they will wait to watch at home, and what kind of movie can still feel like a win. Netflix is still a big part of that story. In its Q1 2026 shareholder letter , the company said re

item.title: RAM prices are turning into an electronics problem
item.description: RAM is not just getting annoying. It is getting expensive in a way that can spill into phones, laptops, PCs, and anything else that needs memory to work. Reuters reported on a global memory shortage forcing AI and consumer-electronics companies to fight for supply, with some segm

item.title: The Xbox and Sony layoffs show why AAA gaming feels broken
item.description: Xbox announced one of the most significant restructures in its history, with about 3,200 roles being reduced through FY27 and four studios leaving Xbox to new management. Microsoft also said it is eliminating around 4,800 roles overall, with the changes mostly falling within Comm

item.title: HubSpot's enrichment backlash is really about first-party data trust
item.description: HubSpot's data enrichment backlash was not really about one feature. It was about who people think owns the data once it lives in the CRM. That is why the reaction got loud so fast. If you run CRM the right way, first-party data is the asset. Notes, deals, lifecycle stages, call

item.title: The World Cup is at its best when the fans matter more than FIFA's money
item.description: One of my favorite stories around this World Cup has nothing to do with a score. It is seeing international soccer fans travel across North America and get genuinely excited about the food. The Associated Press reported on fans trying local food across the United States, Canada,

item.title: Qualification, research, and AEO are how AI becomes normal business
item.description: Harvard Business Review looked at how three small and midsize businesses , including a manufacturer, a boutique hotel, and a B2B software company, are adapting as AI changes how customers research, evaluate, and choose suppliers. I agree with the larger point, but what stands out

item.title: Sony executives selling stock makes the Bungie layoffs look even worse
item.description: Sony's executive stock sales are a bad look, especially when you put them next to what just happened at Bungie. Sony President and CEO Hiroki Totoki disclosed that he sold 225,000 Sony shares on July 3 at $21.02 per share. That comes out to about $4.73 million. The part that stan

item.title: HubSpot's data enrichment reversal is really a trust problem
item.description: HubSpot reversed its planned data enrichment terms change after a pretty loud customer backlash, especially on LinkedIn. Duncan Lennox, HubSpot's Chief Product and Technology Officer, said they got it wrong and would not move forward with the terms of service changes communicated

item.title: Trump's $2.2 billion year is a public trust problem
item.description: The Wall Street Journal reported that President Donald Trump's personal income rose to more than $2.2 billion in the first year of his second term, with a huge portion of that tied to crypto ventures, licensing, investments, and his broader business holdings. A New York Times rep

item.title: SpaceX in retirement accounts is where the hype gets personal
item.description: The Guardian reported that the SpaceX story is starting to reach retirement savings, and that is where the whole thing gets more personal. Once a company becomes something index funds and 401(k)s have to carry, the risk stops being a billionaire game and starts touching ordinary

item.title: Apple's price hike warning shows the AI boom is reaching consumers
item.description: Al Jazeera reported that Tim Cook says price increases for some Apple products are unavoidable because the AI boom is driving memory and storage costs higher. That is the part of the story that should make people pause. I think this is where the AI boom stops being abstract. Peop

item.title: The memory crunch is the part of the AI boom people are ignoring
item.description: Euronews reported that the AI-driven memory crunch is already pushing hardware costs higher. That is the hidden constraint most people skip over when they talk about AI as if it is only a software story. I think this is the real pressure point. Everyone wants to focus on the mode

item.title: The pushback against data centers is the real AI story now
item.description: Business Insider reported that communities are pushing back on data centers and winning some of those fights. I think that is the next phase of the AI infrastructure story. For a while, the industry acted like these projects could just show up anywhere and everyone would call it

item.title: The mega-IPO wave is what happens when the market gets crowded with hype
item.description: Business Insider reported that trader Ed Elson is bearish on the coming mega-IPO wave, including SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, and other big names. I think that is the part worth paying attention to. When too many giant stories hit the market at the same time, the market stops rewar

item.title: Anthropic's compute deal shows AI is turning into balance-sheet infrastructure
item.description: Apollo announced a $35 billion capital solution with Blackstone and Broadcom tied to AI compute infrastructure. That is a very different kind of AI story than a model launch or a product demo. This is AI being financed like infrastructure. I think that is the part worth paying at

item.title: Amazon's new AI loan makes the spending story impossible to ignore
item.description: TechCrunch reported that Amazon has lined up another $17.5 billion loan tied to its AI expansion. That is the kind of number that makes it hard to keep treating AI spending like a normal tech investment cycle. Amazon has a real business underneath this. AWS is not some speculativ

item.title: Texas telling data centers to pay their own way is where this gets real
item.description: Business Insider reported that Texas Governor Greg Abbott wants data centers to pay more of their own way when it comes to power, water, and infrastructure. That is a big signal because Texas is not exactly known for being hostile to business. This is where the data center story

item.title: OpenAI's data center influence warning shows the AI fight is bigger than zoning
item.description: Business Insider reported that OpenAI disrupted a suspected China-linked influence operation trying to shape the conversation around US data centers. That is a strange story on the surface, but it makes sense once you think about where the AI race is heading. Data centers are no

item.title: HubSpot's agentic AI bet is really about whether CRM becomes the operating layer
item.description: Fast Slow Motion's guide to agentic AI in HubSpot gets at the real question for CRM right now: can the CRM become the operating layer for the business, or is it still just the place where teams log activity after the real work happens somewhere else? That is why I think HubSpot's

item.title: Wall Street is flooding the hyperscalers with cash, but the real bill is still ahead
item.description: The Financial Times reported that AI demand is driving a record wave of power and utility dealmaking. I think the hyperscaler investment story is one of the hardest ones to read right now, because the numbers are so large that they almost stop meaning anything on first pass. When

item.title: HubSpot's new AI pricing makes the product story feel more real
item.description: The interesting part of HubSpot's Customer Agent and Prospecting Agent announcement is not just that they exist. It is the way HubSpot is pricing them. That tells you a lot about where the company thinks AI is actually useful. Instead of treating these like shiny add-ons, HubSpot

item.title: HubSpot's Spring 2026 Spotlight is all about making AI actually useful
item.description: HubSpot's Spring 2026 Spotlight reads like a company trying to make one very clear point: AI is only helpful if it knows enough about the business to do something useful with it. That is what I think makes this release more interesting than a normal product update. It is not just

item.title: SpaceX's IPO story is really a story about what the market will pay for momentum
item.description: My actual take on the SpaceX IPO story is simple. SpaceX is a good company. I do think that. But I also think the company is being tied into a much bigger Elon Musk story, and that whole package is being used to chase a trillion-dollar valuation. The SpaceX filing makes the ambit

item.title: Oracle's AI infrastructure rally is about to meet the real world
item.description: Oracle has clearly benefited from the AI infrastructure trade, and I understand why investors have been willing to reward that story. The market loves a clean narrative, and right now the narrative is simple: build data centers, support the AI buildout, and let the demand follow.

item.title: Stargate is the clearest sign that AI is now a utility-scale business
item.description: OpenAI, Oracle, and SoftBank's five-site Stargate expansion is what happens when an AI story stops being about features and starts being about infrastructure. Once you get to five new data centers, you are not talking about software anymore. You are talking about land, power, per

item.title: The AI data center boom is running into a political problem
item.description: I think one of the biggest changes in the AI story right now is that communities are no longer reacting like they did at the beginning of the boom. Business Insider has tracked how data center opposition is showing up around the country. People have seen what these projects can d

item.title: China's space-based AI plan says a lot about where this race is headed
item.description: I do not think space-based AI data centers are the near-term answer for most companies. But I do think the idea says a lot about where the competition is heading. Tom's Hardware reported on China's push toward orbiting AI data centers, and when people start talking seriously abou

item.title: The most valuable AI use case isn't a product. It's a personal tool.
item.description: There is a version of AI that gets a lot of attention and a version that does not. The one that gets the attention is commercial: AI copilots, AI add-ons, AI agents that companies can license. The one that does not is quieter and, honestly, more genuinely useful. It is people bui

item.title: The Bricks & Minifigs Lego mess is a resale-business problem waiting to happen
item.description: There is a serious trust issue in the Bricks & Minifigs story . Once a resale business takes custody of somebody else's inventory, the business is responsible for protecting both the items and the expectation around them. That is where this gets messy. Franchise changes, weak pap

item.title: Costco's gas boom is a lesson in leveraging a great core business
item.description: Costco's gas business works because it reinforces the membership model. The cheap gas is not separate from the warehouse business. It is part of why people keep showing up. That is the point I keep coming back to. A strong core business gives you room to build adjacent economics

item.title: Dell's best day ever tells you where enterprise AI is actually going
item.description: Dell's AI infrastructure story explains why the market reacted so strongly. Dell's stock had its best single day ever on Thursday - up 32%. The reason: AI server revenue. Not AI features. Not AI announcements. Not a partnership press release. Actual infrastructure revenue from en

item.title: Samsung just promised $400K bonuses. That's an AI story too.
item.description: Samsung Electronics' profit-sharing deal could pay semiconductor workers average bonuses of nearly $400,000. The agreement runs ten years and allocates 10.5% of the semiconductor division's operating profit directly to employees. That's a significant number. And it's an interesti

item.title: AI is making cybersecurity harder - and that's a GTM problem too
item.description: CrowdStrike's AI security comments point to a warning worth paying attention to: AI cybersecurity tools are approaching the point where they will outpace human researchers at finding vulnerabilities. AI systems are already accelerating how fast security flaws get discovered - on

item.title: Where AI actually falls apart
item.description: I've been testing Claude, Claude Co-Work, and Claude Dispatch to see how much of my own workload I can actually hand off. The answer is: some of it, but not the parts that depend on memory, state, or judgment. That is where AI falls apart. It can look persistent and helpful, but

item.title: The shift from "AI tools" to AI with actual business context
item.description: One thing I'm watching closely in the HubSpot ecosystem right now is the shift from "AI tools" to AI with actual business context. HubSpot's recent Spring Spotlight updates around AEO, AI agents, and contextual CRM data feel less like simple AI features and more like the beginnin

item.title: HubSpot vs. Salesforce: two very different bets on AI agents
item.description: Salesforce has been loud about what AI means for their workforce. Marc Benioff said they would not hire new engineers this year because AI is doing the work. They've since cut 4,000 customer service roles. The message is pretty clear: agents in, people out. HubSpot is taking a di

item.title: HubSpot's AI push is a signal every RevOps team should read
item.description: HubSpot continuing to invest into AI-powered automation is a reminder that CRM platforms are turning into operational systems, not just databases of record. For RevOps teams, this matters. The tools are getting smarter, but the strategy still has to come from people who understan

item.title: Site is live, built and deployed with AI
item.description: I built this entire site using Claude Code , Anthropic's AI coding tool. Start to finish: under an hour. GitHub repo, GitHub Pages deployment, custom workflow, all of it. The workflow is simple: describe what you want, review the output, push. No boilerplate hunting, no getting l

item.title: What 10 years in marketing ops actually teaches you
item.description: I've spent over 10 years helping organizations market themselves online through SamCBarth.com : generating leads, tracking attribution, managing CRMs, and turning raw data into reporting that actually drives decisions. The answer is always the same: garbage in, garbage out. The b
